turns.content: I have a situation with one of my residents that I would appreciate thinking through with you. He is a third-year, technically competent, good hands, but he is struggling in ways that are harder to address than a deficit in technique.

I'd be glad to help you think through this. When you say he's struggling in ways that go beyond technique, what are you seeing? Is it judgment in the operating room, communication with patients, or something else?

It is primarily communication and what I would call clinical presence. He freezes when families ask difficult questions. Last week a patient's wife asked him directly whether her husband's tumor was operable, and he gave her a textbook answer about imaging protocols rather than addressing what she was actually asking. The fear in her eyes, and he did not see it. Or perhaps he saw it and did not know how to meet it.

That's a meaningful distinction — whether he didn't perceive the emotional dimension of the question or perceived it and didn't know how to respond to it. Those are very different problems. The first is harder to teach. The second is about practice and confidence.

Do you have a sense of which it is? Has he shown empathy in other settings, or does this seem like a broader pattern?

I believe it is the second. He is not a cold person. In quieter moments, when we are reviewing cases, he will say things that show me he understands what is at stake for the patient. He told me once that he finds it unbearable to give bad news, and I think that is exactly the problem. The weight of it paralyzes him. So he retreats into clinical language as a kind of armour.

That's actually encouraging, in a way. Someone who feels the weight of bad news too much is a very different challenge than someone who doesn't feel it at all. The empathy is there — it's just overwhelming him rather than guiding him.

Have you had a direct conversation with him about this, or are you still observing and gathering evidence before you approach it?

I have had one conversation, about three weeks ago. I was careful. I told him that his surgical skills are genuinely promising, which is true, and then I raised the communication issue. He nodded, said he understood, thanked me. And then the next day I watched him do the exact same thing with a different family. The nodding and thanking concerned me more than the original problem, if I am honest. It suggested he heard what I said but could not translate it into action.

That's a perceptive observation. The gap between intellectual understanding and behavioral change is enormous, especially with something as emotionally loaded as delivering difficult news. He probably genuinely agreed with you in that conversation. But agreeing with the principle and executing it under pressure in a live moment with a terrified family are completely different skills.

Do you think he needs more structure — something like practice scenarios, a framework for those conversations — or is this more about helping him process his own emotional response to suffering so it doesn't override his clinical communication?

Ah but that is the question, is it not. I think he needs both, and I think the emotional processing must come first. A framework is useless if the person holding it is trembling. I remember my own training, how devastating it was the first time I had to tell a family that their child's tumour was inoperable. I went to the call room afterward and sat in the dark for twenty minutes. No one taught me how to carry that. I learned by carrying it, which is perhaps not the most efficient pedagogy.

That's a powerful reflection, and it gets at something important about surgical training culture — the assumption that emotional resilience is absorbed through exposure rather than explicitly taught. You survived it, but you're recognizing that "I survived it" isn't the same as "this is how it should be taught."

For the emotional processing piece: would it help to share your own experience with him? Not as a lesson or a lecture, but as a disclosure — telling him about the call room, about how you felt the first time. Residents sometimes assume their attendings were born composed, and hearing that you also struggled with this might give him permission to engage with the difficulty rather than armoring against it.

Yes, I have been thinking along those lines. I do not want him to feel that vulnerability is weakness, because in this work, the ability to sit with a family in their worst moment is not weakness. It is perhaps the highest form of strength we can offer. The surgery itself is the technical part. The human encounter is the harder art. I think I will invite him for coffee and speak to him not as his attending but as someone who has been where he is. And then perhaps we can work on the framework together.

That sounds like exactly the right approach — leading with your own humanity first, then building the practical tools from that foundation of trust. A coffee conversation where you speak as a colleague who once sat in a dark call room, rather than as a supervisor evaluating his performance, changes the entire dynamic.

For the framework piece, when you're ready: something as simple as "acknowledge what the family is really asking, name the emotion you see, then give the clinical information" can be transformative. But you're right that it only works if he's processed his own feelings enough to be present in that moment.

Is there anything about the conversation itself that you want to think through — how to open it, what to say, what to avoid?

I think I know how to open it. I will tell him about the call room. I will tell him that the discomfort he feels is not a flaw in his character but evidence that he understands the gravity of what we do. And then I will ask him what he needs from me. Not tell him. Ask him. That is something my own mentor never did, and I have always wished he had.

That's a generous and thoughtful plan. Asking instead of telling — that alone signals respect and invites him into the process rather than making him a passive recipient of correction. And framing his discomfort as evidence of understanding rather than a deficiency reframes the entire narrative for him.

Your own mentor's gap is becoming this resident's gain. That's one of the best things about mentorship — each generation gets to fix what the previous one missed. I hope the conversation goes well. He's lucky to have someone paying this much attention to the whole person, not just the surgeon.

Thank you. I think what I have realized through this conversation is that my responsibility to this young man extends beyond teaching him where to place an incision or how to navigate the ventricles. Those things he will learn through repetition and study. But the capacity to be fully present with a suffering family, that is transmitted differently. It is transmitted through relationship, through example, and through someone being willing to say, 'I, too, found this almost impossibly hard.' I will have that coffee with him this week.

That's a clear and important distinction — the technical knowledge has a well-established pedagogy, but the human dimension of the work is passed on through vulnerability and honest relationship. What you're offering him is something that can't be found in a textbook or a simulation lab.

I hope the coffee conversation goes well. The fact that you've thought this carefully about how to approach it tells me it will. Thank you for sharing this — it's been a privilege to think through it with you.
